macro_segments.title: Rabbi Tarfon's Position on Full vs. Half Damages in the Owner's Property

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Tarfon's Kal Vachomer Argument Comparing Shen/Regel to Keren
The Rabbis' Response Using the Dayo Principle
Rabbi Tarfon's Alternative Approach Focusing on Reshut Hanizak
The Rabbis' Rebuttal That Keren Remains Limited by Dayo

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Analysis: Does Rabbi Tarfon Hold the Dayo Principle

micro_segments.title: The Gemara Questions Whether Rabbi Tarfon Accepts the Dayo Principle
Introduction of Dayo as a Biblical Principle from the Thirteen Hermeneutic Rules
The Biblical Source: Miriam's Punishment and the Seven-Day Principle
Explanation of How Dayo Limits Kal Vachomer in the Miriam Case
Rabbi Tarfon's Position: When Dayo Undermines Kal Vachomer, It Does Not Apply
Detailed Explanation of Tarfon's Principle Using the Goring Ox Example
The Rabbis' Counter-Response: Dayo Is Always Applied and the Seven-Day Rule Applies
Rabbi Tarfon's Response: The Repetition in the Pasuk Teaches That Dayo Is a General Principle

macro_segments.title: Rav Pappa's Challenge: A Tanna Who Rejects Dayo Even When Kal Vachomer Bears Fruit

micro_segments.title: Rav Pappa Presents a Case of Keri B'Zav Where Dayo Is Not Applied
The Kal Vachomer Regarding Semen of a Regular Person vs. a Zav
The Halacha: Semen of a Zav Is Tamei Even by Carrying, Not Just Touching
The Gemara's Question: Why Is Dayo Not Applied When Kal Vachomer Still Produces Results
The Answer: The Source Pasuk Already Taught That All Bodily Fluids of a Zav Are Tamei
The Mechanics: Semen Was Already Known to Be Tamei by Touch for All People
The Comparison: Neither Rabbi Eliezer nor Rabbi Yehoshua Hold the Braita's Position
The Mishna from Kelim: Zav's Fluids Are All Tamei by Both Touching and Carrying
Confirmation: The Braita Represents the Kal Vachomer Deriving the Law Without Dayo Limitations

macro_segments.title: Rav Acha's Challenge: A Tanna Who Rejects Dayo Even When Kal Vachomer Bears Fruit

micro_segments.title: Rav Acha Mi'Difti Presents the Case of Mapatz (Mat) and Meis (Corpse)
The Kal Vachomer: Small Vessels Are Tahor by Zav but Tamei by Sheretz
Why Small Vessels Cannot Become Tamei by Zav vs. How Meis Transmits Tumah
The Kal Vachomer: Mapatz Tamei by Zav Should Certainly Be Tamei by Meis
The Problem: Dayo Would Limit Mapatz by Meis to One Day, Not Seven Days
Ravina's Response: The Law Is Actually Learned From Sheretz First, Using a Gezerah Shavah
Mapatz by Sheretz: Another Kal Vachomer Showing Small Vessels Are Tamei by Sheretz
The Gezerah Shavah Between Sheretz and Meis Using the Words Beged V'Or
Analysis of Beged V'Or as Extra Words to Strengthen the Gezerah Shavah
The Connection Between Sheretz and Semen in the Same Pasuk
How the Extra Words on Both Sides of the Gezerah Shavah Make It Ironclad
The Connection Between Meis and Semen in the Same Pasuk
Resolving the Question of How to Achieve Seven Days: Don Minah V'Okei B'Atrah
The Final Pasuk: Kibastem Bigdeichem Shows Seven Days Applies to All Tamei by Meis
